english: tan nyid (tan nyid) Tar. 15, 14 id.; dé-nas mi riṅ-ba-nyid-na (de nas mi ring ba nyid na) a very short time after Tar.; when added to adjectives it denotes abstract nouns, as in English the terminations: -ness, -ship, -ty, -cy, -y etc., but it is chiefly limited to the language of philosophical writings, from which a few expressions only (such as stoṅ-pa-nyid (stong pa nyid) the emptiness, the Buddhist vacuum) have found their way into col. language. — 2. In the more recent literature it is used resp. for kyod (khyod), thou, you; nyid-kyi (nyid kyi) thy, your Pth., Ma.; nyid - raṅ (nyid rang) you (col. *nyi - raṅ (nyi rang), nyo-raṅ* (nyo rang)) W., C., resp., like the German 'Sie'; nyid-cag(-raṅ) (nyid cag rang) you, addressed to one person or to several, C. (in Glr. kyed-cag (khyed cag) seems to be used in the same way). — 3. only, graṅs-kyi lṅa nyid (grangs kyi lnga nyid) Zam. only the numeral lṅa; za nyid-do (za nyid do) the letter za alone (without a prefix).

1. day, = nyi-ma (nyi ma) 2; nyin-gyi riṅ - la (nyin gyi ring la) during the day-time Pth.; nyin-mor gyur (nyin mor gyur) it dawns Cs.; nyin-mor byed (nyin mor byed) 'making day', an epithet of the sun Cs.; nyin (nyin) adv. in the day-time Glr.; nyin-cig (nyin cig) one day, once Dzl.; nyin cig bźin-du (nyin cig bzhin du) daily Dzl.; nyin-par (nyin par) during the day-time Dzl.; by day-light Dzl.; deï nyin-par (de'i nyin par) on that day, frq. Dzl.; pyir nyin (phyir nyin), pyi de nyin (phyi de nyin), deï pyi nyin (de'i phyi nyin) the following day, on the f.d. Dzl.; tses bcò-lṅaï nyin (tshes bco lnga'i nyin) the 15th., on the 15th. Glr.; fig.: bstán-pa nyin-par mdzád-paï skyés-bu (bstan pa nyin par mdzad pa'i skyes bu) a saint that restores the doctrine, a reformer of faith; hence Schr.: dád-paï nyin-byed (dad pa'i nyin byed) evangelist, apostle. — 2. propitious day; *ṅa ća nyin-mo mi dug* (nga ca nyin mo mi dug) W. this day is not propitious for me to go. Comp. nyin-dkár (nyin dkar) a white, a lucky day Sch. — nyin-gáṅ (nyin gang), nyin-ṭog-ṭág (nyin thog thag) (W. *ṭag-ṭog* (thog thag)) all the day long. — nyin-gúṅ (nyin gung) noon. — nyin-gla (nyin gla) daily pay, a day's hire Cs. — *nyin-tse-ré* (nyin tse re) W. all the day long, the live-long day. — nyin-mtsán (nyin mtshan) 1. a day and a night, nyin-mtsán bcò-brgyád (nyin mtshan bco brgyad) Mil. for nine days and nine nights. 2. day and night Dzl., nyin-mtsan-méd-par (nyin mtshan med par) id., frq.; nyin-med-tsán-med (nyin med mtshan med) W. id.; nyin-mtsán-du (nyin mtshan du) id. Mil.; nyin-mtsán mnyám-pa (nyin mtshan mnyam pa) equinox: — nyin-źág(-gcig) (nyin zhag gcig) 1. a day with the night, 24 hours, divided into 12 portions of time, called kyim (khyim) (q.v.): nam-pyéd (nam phyed) midnight, nam-pyed-yól (nam phyed yol) 2 o'clock a.m., to-ráṅs (tho rangs) 4 o'cl. a.m. (in popular language also: *j́a-po dáṅ-po* (bya po dang po) about 2 o'cl., *nyis-pa* (gnyis pa) 3 o'cl., *súm-pa* (gsum pa) 4 o'cl., nam-laṅs (nam langs) 6 o'cl a. m. (i.e. the time when the sun first illumines the mountain tops; it is from this moment, and not from midnight, that in daily life the date is counted); nyi-śár (nyi shar) 8 o'cl. a. m. (when the sun rises upon the valley); dros-jám (dros byam) (col. *nyi-dúl* (nyi dul)) 10 o'cl. a. m.; nyin-gúṅ (nyin gung), nyi-pyéd (nyi phyed) 12 o'cl., noon; pyed-ćól (phyed tsol) (W. *zá-ra ṗi-mo* (za ra phyi mo)) 2 o'cl. p. m., myur-smád (myur smad) 4 o'cl. p. m., nyi-rgás (nyi rgas) 6 o'cl. p. m., srod-kór (srod 'khor) 8 o'cl. p. m. (col. *sa-rúb (sa rub), srod-rúb* (srod rub)), srod-ćól (srod tsol) 10 o'cl. p. m. (col. *tiṅ-nyi* (ting nyi)) — thus acc. to Wdk. By adding the names of the 12 years' cycle (nam-pyéd byi-ba (nam phyed byi ba), pyed-ćól glaṅ (phyed tsol glang) etc., v. the word lo (lo)), these terms have been rendered still more convenient for astrological calculations. Of course, all the terms given are strictly correct only at the time of the equinoxes, and deviate at the summer and winter solstices for more than an hour from the time indicated by our clocks. 2. nyin-źág (nyin zhag) as symb. num.: 15. — nyiṅ-bźin-gyis (nyin bzhin gyis) Pth., nyin-ré bźin (nyin re bzhin) Glr., daily adv., with -gyi (-gyi) adj. — nyin-lám (nyin lam) a day's journey Glr., rkaṅ-táṅ-gi (rkang thang gi), rtá-pai (rta pa'i), lúg-pai nyin-lam (lug pa'i nyin lam) a pedestrian's, a horseman's, a sheep-driver's daily march. — nyin-ráṅs (nyin rangs) Tar. (= to-ráṅs (tho rangs)) day-break, morning twilight Schf.
W. *nyib-ćóg(s)* (nyib tsogs) the sunny side of mountains.
to decay, to crumble to pieces, of rocks, mountains etc.; rarely to run down, of tears, to flow down, of locks of hair.
1. instrum. of nyi (nyi). — 2. in compounds for gnyis (gnyis).
